title: Colony Ship: A Post-Earth Role Playing Game

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Colony Ship as an 'isometric, party-based RPG' with turn-based play, a detailed skill-based character system, multiple ways to handle quests, branching dialogue trees, and choices-and-consequences faction negotiation aboard a generation ship. That combination strongly fits a party-based CRPG: isometric perspective, party control, authored branching quest structure, tactical/methodical turn-based encounters, and heavy dialogue-choice driven progression. Secondary overlap with western_narrative_rpg and tactical_rpg is present, but the party-based isometric CRPG identity is the clearest core gameplay profile.

overall_note: The set captures Colony Ship's old-school isometric CRPG identity fairly well, especially its tactical turn-based combat and reactivity. The main gap is that several candidates drift toward solo-character or more combat-forward/open-world structures, while Colony Ship is specifically a party-based, choice-heavy CRPG.
anchor_summary: Colony Ship: A Post-Earth Role Playing Game is a hardcore isometric party-based CRPG centered on turn-based tactical combat, skill-driven character building, branching quests with multiple solutions, dense dialogue checks, faction reactivity, and single-player campaign progression.
gap_notes: A stronger rail would prioritize party-based isometric CRPGs with heavy quest reactivity and dialogue checks.
Some candidates match the hardcore turn-based RPG feel but miss the anchor's party-management focus.
The set leans a bit broad between classic reactive CRPGs and more tactics/survival-oriented RPGs.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on party-based isometric structure, turn-based tactical combat, squad builds, faction choices, and reactive questing, though it is broader and more combat/tactics-forward than Colony Ship.
A strong sci-fi isometric CRPG match with skill checks, branching quests, faction play, exploration, and tactical turn-based encounters; overall structure is highly compatible with Colony Ship.

Extremely close in hardcore skill-based quest design, consequences, dialogue density, and punishing turn-based combat, but less aligned on party-based play since it is primarily a solo-character CRPG.

Good fit on isometric squad-based turn-based combat and narrative choice, but its hub-and-mission campaign structure is more streamlined and less systemic than Colony Ship's classic reactive CRPG design.

Matches the harsh old-school isometric turn-based RPG feel, difficult build-driven combat, and factional survival setting, but it is more solo, exploration/combat-centric, and less party-based/dialogue-reactive than Colony Ship.

title: Terra Invicta

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Terra Invicta as a long-form strategic campaign centered on faction-level control of Earth's nations, covert political influence, global research direction, proxy conflicts, Solar System expansion, and base/station construction. The text repeatedly emphasizes geopolitics as a sandbox, detailed regional simulation, councilor management, control points, colonization of asteroids/moons/planets, and tactical fleet combat. That macro-scale political and imperial management profile fits grand_strategy best, with strong 4X overlap from expansion, resource extraction, research, and space colonization. Evidence is rich and concrete across Steam, OpenCritic, and Metacritic-derived stored text, not merely genre tags.

overall_note: The rail has a solid strategic-space center of gravity and mostly stays within meaningful macro-strategy neighbors. Its main gap is that several candidates capture only one side of Terra Invicta well—either the space 4X layer or the geopolitical grand-strategy/intrigue layer—rather than the full Earth-to-space hybrid identity.
anchor_summary: Terra Invicta is a hybrid grand strategy/4X game built around covert faction control of Earth, geopolitical manipulation, research direction, councilor operations, Solar System expansion, base construction, and tactical fleet combat in a long-form single-player campaign. The closest comps should match macro-scale strategic management, factional politics, expansion/progression, and space warfare more than just sharing sci-fi or strategy labeling.
gap_notes: No candidate fully matches Terra Invicta's distinctive mix of covert faction politics on Earth plus later Solar System colonization and tactical fleet combat.
The set slightly over-indexes on traditional space 4X and under-represents games with comparable shadow-politics, control-point, or councilor-style operational play.
Historical grand strategy neighbors are valid, but they should rank below the strongest space-strategy comps because Terra Invicta's expansion and fleet layer is central, not incidental.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Best overall comp: pausable macro sci-fi strategy with research, diplomacy, expansion, colony/station management, and fleet warfare over a long campaign, though it lacks Terra Invicta's Earthside covert-politics structure.
Very strong on grand-strategy pacing, ideology, geopolitics, research, military buildup, and pausable macro management; weaker on exploration, colonization, and the space-4X side.

Strong space-strategy match through faction asymmetry, colonization, tech progression, empire management, and fleet conflict, but its turn-based empire loop is more classical 4X and less like Terra Invicta's geopolitical simulation.

Reasonable neighbor for research, diplomacy, colonization, and galactic political influence, but it is a cleaner traditional 4X empire-builder with less overlap in covert control, Earth politics, and tactical campaign texture.

Shares indirect control, intrigue, long-form political maneuvering, and grand-strategy scale, but its character-dynasty roleplay loop is much farther from Terra Invicta's modern geopolitical and space-expansion gameplay.

title: ARMA 3 Contact

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as a spin-off expansion built in 'authentic Arma 3 style,' centered on a military science-fiction campaign with combat recon, electronic warfare, new factions, weapons, vehicles, and a large sandbox terrain suited for combined arms warfare. Store labels also confirm single-player, co-op, and PvP support. The strongest gameplay signals are tactical realism, combat-focused military operations, open sandbox terrain, and methodical shooter play, which fit tactical_shooter best; military_fps is a close secondary fit.

overall_note: The set is directionally strong because all three candidates target realism-minded tactical military shooting, but it misses the obvious parent/base-game pairing and leans too much on neighbors when the anchor is explicitly an ARMA 3 expansion.
anchor_summary: ARMA 3 Contact is a DLC/spin-off expansion built on ARMA 3’s military sandbox: first-person/combined-arms mil-sim gunplay, large open terrain, vehicles, recon, tactical pacing, and simulation-heavy mission structure, with a more authored sci-fi campaign layer on top.
gap_notes: A direct ARMA 3 base-game entry is the clearest must-have comp for a Contact rail, but it is not actually present in the candidate set.
Arma 3 Apex is very close in gameplay identity as a sibling expansion, but it is not the anchor's base game despite the requested relationship metadata.
Squad shares tactical infantry realism and combined arms, but its identity is much more multiplayer match-driven than Contact's authored expansion/campaign structure.

candidate_reviews.rationale: A sibling ARMA 3 expansion with essentially the same core mil-sim sandbox, weapons/vehicles, large-terrain operations, and mission feel; not the base game, but still an essential rail inclusion.
Very close on simulation-heavy military sandbox, realistic infantry combat, combined arms, large maps, and modding, though it is its own platform/game mix rather than ARMA 3 expansion content.

Strong overlap in tactical realism, coordination, vehicles, and long-range combined-arms firefights, but it is primarily a large-scale multiplayer session shooter rather than a campaign/sandbox expansion in the ARMA mold.

title: RimWorld - Royalty

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: This is additive DLC for RimWorld and does not contradict the parent game's core identity, so the parent gameplay should drive classification. Parent text explicitly describes RimWorld as a 'sci-fi colony sim' where you manage colonists' needs and relationships, build and craft a settlement, trade, tame animals, form caravans, and respond to raids and other systemic threats. Royalty adds quests, psychic powers, noble titles, throne rooms, hospitality scenarios, and more combat/event variety, but these extend the same colony-management and story-generation loop rather than establishing a different genre. The combined evidence strongly supports colony_sim with systemic sandbox management, emergent narrative, settlement building, colony growth, and tactical encounter handling.

overall_note: This candidate set is well targeted for a colony-sim anchor and correctly includes the essential parent game. The top of the rail is especially strong, though the lower end drifts toward broader management/building sims that share interface or construction DNA more than the full RimWorld-style colony-survival-combat loop.
anchor_summary: RimWorld - Royalty is additive DLC for RimWorld, so the anchor’s gameplay identity is fundamentally a top-down colony sim: managing individual colonists’ needs and traits, building and expanding a settlement, handling production/survival, and responding to emergent threats and tactical combat. Royalty adds quests, noble systems, psychic abilities, and more event/combat variety, but it does not change the core loop away from RimWorld’s story-driven colony management sandbox.
gap_notes: RimWorld should be the clear #1 because Royalty is direct DLC and the most expected pairing by far.
Dwarf Fortress, Stranded: Alien Dawn, and Going Medieval all fit the colony-management/survival sandbox well, with Dwarf Fortress standing out as a core benchmark.
Prison Architect is understandable due to top-down construction/management and emergent incidents, but it is materially looser because it lacks the same colony-survival-adventure/tactical identity.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct parent game for this DLC; same core colony-sim systems, perspective, progression, and audience expectation.
One of the clearest genre benchmarks: emergent colony stories, pawn simulation, fortress building, crisis management, and systemic survival are extremely close.
Very similar pawn-needs settlement management loop with building, crafting, raid defense, and sandbox survival structure; more architectural/3D but still a strong fit.
Small-group sci-fi survival colony sim with base building, survivor management, research, and defense against external threats; slightly less socially/systemically rich than RimWorld.
Shares top-down construction, staff/inmate scheduling, and emergent management crises, but lacks RimWorld’s expedition, survival, character-story, and tactical-combat mix.

title: Total War: THREE KINGDOMS - Eight Princes

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted as grand_strategy using DLC inheritance from the parent game Total War: THREE KINGDOMS, since Eight Princes is clearly an additive chapter pack rather than a genre-shifting mode. The DLC text repeatedly describes new campaign mechanics, princes, buildings, assignments, court options, faction advancement, alignments, and elite units, which matches the parent game's core loop. Parent evidence is strong and explicit: THREE KINGDOMS combines a turn-based campaign of empire-building, statecraft, and conquest with real-time battles. That supports grand_strategy as the primary identity, with rts as a secondary flavor for battlefield engagements. Historical setting, long-form campaign structure, systemic faction management, settlement development, and tactical optimization are all directly supported by the stored descriptions.

overall_note: The rail is strong and correctly centers the base game first, then other close Total War hybrids that share the same turn-based campaign plus real-time battle identity. Ordering is broadly sensible, with historical Total War entries fitting slightly better than the fantasy variant.
anchor_summary: Total War: THREE KINGDOMS - Eight Princes is a chapter-pack DLC for a hybrid Total War strategy game: turn-based grand-campaign empire management, diplomacy, character/court systems, settlement development, and real-time tactical battles. Best matches should preserve that exact campaign-plus-battle loop, long-form faction progression, and primarily single-player strategy structure.
gap_notes: Base game inclusion is essential for a DLC anchor and is correctly present.
Historical Total War titles fit a bit better than Warhammer II because they more closely match campaign texture, faction politics, and army composition expectations, not just the series formula.

title: Battle Cry of Freedom

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Battle Cry of Freedom as a large-scale multiplayer PvP combat game centered on historically grounded Civil War battles with first- and third-person gunplay, artillery, cavalry, engineer support, melee bayonet/sabre fighting, realistic projectile physics, destructible battlefields, and commander-led tactical play. The strongest identity is tactical shooter: match-based battlefield engagements, realism-leaning weapon behavior, role-based military units, and emphasis on tactical cunning over narrative or RPG progression. It also overlaps with military_fps due to historical armed combat and firearm focus, but the scale, role variety, and tactical realism make tactical_shooter the better primary fit. Evidence for MMO-like scale exists in store genres and 500-600 player battles, but not enough to justify an MMO RPG archetype.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's realism-leaning battlefield combat and large-battle sensibility reasonably well, but it leans either toward sandbox action-RPG structure or smaller-scale trench FPS rather than closely matching Battle Cry of Freedom's line-battle, role-based, combined-arms multiplayer identity.

title: AI War 2

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify AI War 2 as a 'grand strategy/RTS hybrid' centered on galactic-scale territorial expansion, fleet and defense-network management, multiple factions with unique economies and rules, and real-time simulation with pause/speed controls. The core loop is long-form campaign play where the player starts from a tiny foothold, expands carefully, manages fronts, optimizes empire and fleet composition, and eventually strikes key AI leaders. The text repeatedly emphasizes macro decision-making, automation, tactical optimization, and large-scale systemic simulation rather than match-based or action-oriented play, which strongly fits grand_strategy with RTS and 4X overlap.

overall_note: The pair captures the anchor's space grand-strategy side, but the set leans more toward broad 4X/grand strategy than AI War 2's specific real-time asymmetrical war-management identity. Distant Worlds: Universe is the stronger fit because of its pausable real-time simulation and automation-heavy macro play.

title: Killer7

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Killer7 as a single-player, story-driven action-adventure with a deeply authored linear campaign, a dark surreal tone, puzzle-solving, character-switching, and hybrid first/third-person shooting encounters. The evidence clearly supports linear progression, encounter-based ranged combat, environmental/puzzle elements, and a strong authored narrative. It does not cleanly match shooter-specialist archetypes like military_fps, tactical_shooter, or third_person_cover_shooter, and the horror is present more as tone and imagery than as pure survival-horror structure, so cinematic_action_adventure is the best fit with action_horror as a secondary influence.

overall_note: The rail captures Killer7's linear authored campaign and stylized action-horror adjacency reasonably well, but most candidates miss its very distinctive hybrid structure: constrained movement, personality-switching, puzzle-solving, and adventure-game eccentricity matter as much as the shooting.
anchor_summary: Killer7 is a highly authored single-player action-adventure with linear progression, unusual on-rails/navigation structure, encounter-based shooting, puzzle gating, character-switching, and a surreal, stylized presentation. Its identity sits closer to experimental cinematic action-adventure with shooter elements than to conventional free-movement shooters or pure survival horror.
gap_notes: The set underrepresents games with similarly unconventional shooter-adventure structure and puzzle-forward progression.
Several candidates match auteur style or surreal presentation better than they match Killer7's actual play pattern.
No candidate closely reflects Killer7's signature character-switching and rail-like traversal constraints.

title: Juno: New Origins

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define Juno: New Origins as a 3D aerospace sandbox centered on constructing and testing rockets, planes, cars, and planets with realistic physics. The core loop is design/build/test/iterate, with deep vehicle customization, orbital mechanics, automation scripting, persistent sandbox play, and a career layer of contracts, milestones, money, and tech-tree unlocks. There is no combat emphasis; the challenge comes from simulation realism and engineering optimization. This strongly fits engineering_sandbox_sim.

overall_note: The set contains the key benchmark and several understandable engineering-sandbox neighbors, but only two candidates strongly match Juno’s aerospace/orbital program identity. The rest drift toward broader construction sandboxes, aircraft-only design, or programmable robotics rather than the anchor’s specific rocket-and-orbit sim loop.
anchor_summary: Juno: New Origins is an engineering-heavy aerospace sandbox sim focused on designing custom vehicles, testing them under realistic flight/orbital physics, iterating on builds, and optionally progressing through contracts, milestones, and a tech tree. The closest matches should share the build-test-iterate vehicle engineering loop and aerospace/orbital simulation identity more than just general sandbox creativity.
gap_notes: Kerbal Space Program is the essential benchmark and should anchor any rail for Juno: New Origins.
Kerbal Space Program 2 is also a very close fit despite its different content/state, because the intended gameplay identity is nearly identical.
SimplePlanes is a strong neighbor due to shared creator DNA and vehicle design/testing loop, but it is notably narrower and less orbital/space-program focused.
Space Engineers overlaps on engineering and spacecraft construction, but survival, mining, combat, destructibility, and multiplayer shift it away from Juno’s sim-program structure.
Main Assembly is a weaker comp: strong creation/programming overlap, but its robot/obstacle-course sandbox is much farther from aerospace/orbital simulation.

title: Amazing Cultivation Simulator

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Official store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify Amazing Cultivation Simulator as a strategy sim/management game inspired by RimWorld and Dwarf Fortress. Core play centers on designing and expanding a cultivation sect, managing disciples, researching knowledge, building infrastructure, handling commerce/political relations, and surviving a procedurally generated world with changing NPC sect interactions. Combat and threats exist (spirits, monsters, demons, beast sieges), but they support a broader settlement/colony-management loop rather than defining the game as an action RPG. The strongest fit in the taxonomy is colony_sim, with management_tycoon and city_builder as secondary influences.

overall_note: The rail has the right genre center of gravity. RimWorld and Dwarf Fortress are essential anchors, with Going Medieval as a solid modern neighbor. anchor_summary: Amazing Cultivation Simulator is best understood as a colony sim/management sandbox: you build and expand a sect, manage disciples' work and needs, research systems, develop infrastructure, interact with factions, and survive procedural threats. Its closest comps should emphasize emergent colony management, simulation depth, base-building, and world/faction systems over direct character-led RPG adventuring or pure survival framing.
gap_notes: RimWorld and Dwarf Fortress should clearly lead this set; both are explicitly foundational references for the anchor's design.

title: Insurgency

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Insurgency as a tactical FPS centered on lethal close-quarters firefights, realistic weapon handling, suppression, no crosshair, squad roles, territorial/objective modes, and mission-based cooperative play. Metacritic explicitly labels it 'Tactical FPS,' while the text repeatedly emphasizes competitive multiplayer, co-op objective missions, one-life tension, teamwork, and realistic combat across modern conflict environments in the Middle East, Africa, and Central Asia. This strongly supports tactical_shooter over broader shooter archetypes.

overall_note: The candidate set is well targeted around hardcore tactical FPS games. It includes the obvious sequel, a very close sibling built on nearly the same framework, and a few broader realism-focused multiplayer shooters that still make sense as neighbors. The main gap is that the lower-ranked options drift toward larger-scale battlefield simulation and away from Insurgency’s tighter CQB/objective-and-co-op identity.
anchor_summary: Insurgency is a hardcore tactical FPS centered on lethal close-quarters gunfights, minimal HUD, realistic weapon handling, squad roles, objective-based PvP, and mission-based co-op against AI. Its identity is a compact, round/session-driven tactical shooter rather than a large-scale milsim or progression-heavy live-service shooter.
gap_notes: Best fits are games with compact round-based tactical gunplay, high lethality, limited HUD, and objective/co-op structure; the top two candidates match that strongly.
The weakest fit in this set is still understandable, but larger-scale combined-arms design and battlefield logistics are meaningfully different from Insurgency’s tighter infantry-focused loop.
A rail built from this set would benefit from keeping emphasis on tactical FPS peers rather than broader milsim titles.

title: Let It Die: Inferno

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a combat-forward third-person action RPG loop centered on descending into Hell Gate, fighting monsters and hostile humans, collecting randomized loot, crafting gear from materials, storing recovered equipment, and permanently improving via retained masteries across seasonal resets. The corpus explicitly calls out 'Melee Action,' separate left/right-hand weapon use, guard breaks, deathblows, randomized enemy placements and loot, randomized weapon/armor traits, and duo co-op plus PvP. Horror tone and setting are strong, but the core identity is better captured as a loot-driven action RPG rather than a pure horror archetype.

overall_note: The rail has one essential inclusion and several credible hardcore action-RPG neighbors, but it leans a bit too much toward general masocore action RPGs rather than games that also capture the anchor's scavenging, loss-recovery, and run-based loot structure.
anchor_summary: LET IT DIE: INFERNO appears to be a combat-forward third-person loot action RPG built around descending a hostile vertical dungeon, surviving high-lethality melee encounters, scavenging randomized gear and materials, crafting and stashing equipment, and progressing through persistent mastery gains despite harsh death loss and seasonal resets. Duo co-op and PvP are meaningful secondary hooks, while horror flavor supports rather than defines the core identity.
gap_notes: Let It Die is an obvious base-game benchmark and should anchor the rail.
Nioh 2 is the best non-franchise comp thanks to lethal melee combat, heavy loot/build depth, and co-op.

The set underrepresents games with stronger extraction-like loss pressure, randomized loot runs, or more direct survival-scavenge structure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify a hybrid of side-scrolling personal combat and strategy/base-building: the player directly fights in 2D battles while issuing troop orders, managing formations, building strongholds, recruiting units, and planning expansion. The Steam detailed copy also establishes a roguelite run structure with branching map progression, loss-on-death resets, and permanent unlocks for future runs. This strongly matches side_scrolling_action_strategy over pure action-platformer, RTS, or management archetypes.

title: American Truck Simulator - Colorado
